-- 2 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 3
Notes
Introduction
This Blueprint will help you to create a campaign in order to win back
customers you can’t afford to lose! And now let’s hear from Bill Glazer:
Bill: The Lost Customer Reactivation Blueprint.
The top six reasons why customers don't return.
Reason number six is price. You're too expensive. What's really important
about that is it's not reason one, two, three, four or five. See most people think
the reason why people don't come back is because you are too expensive and
it is in the top six, but it's number six in the top six. So price is not the reason
why most people don't come back.
Next one is move away. A lot of people seem to eat close to where they live, so
people move away. So that's reason number five.
Reason number four is lousy service. They just weren't treated correctly.
Reason number three is a lousy product. They were given a product that they
were unhappy with.

-- 3 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 4
Notes
Reason number two is they found somebody new.
Reason number one; however, is the fact that they just forgot. They forgot to
come back. And which is really interesting about this is because if you look at
the six top reasons why people don't return, which is the one that is most in
your control in order to get them to return?
Audience: Number one.
Bill: Number one. That's in your control because here's the deal. It's not your
customer's job to remember to do business with you; it's your job to remind
them. Because we're all busy people, we're thinking about our own business.
We're thinking about our own families. We're thinking about all the things that
are going on in our life. We're not thinking about others. The number one
reason why they don't come back is not because they're unhappy with the
products and service that you deliver to them it's just that they forgot. You're
not on their mind.
The important thing about this is that unless you invite them to return, many
people will not return because they are embarrassed about it.

-- 4 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 5
Notes
So to Dan's point there's two pieces about this. Piece number one is you need
to 	invite 	them 	back, 	and 	piece 	number 	two 	is 	the 	fact 	that 	they 	are
embarrassed in most cases, which is going to hold them back from coming. So
the invitation when you do that takes away the embarrassment.
So who has lost customers in their business? As a matter of fact, just about
every business that you actually work with has lost customers in them. Maybe
the only exception to that is if you're in the funeral business because once you
lose that person it's hard to bury them again.

-- 5 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 6
Notes
So lost customers. 	Let's talk about what lost customers are so that we define
it. 	Customers who have bought from you in the past, but not return when
expected. 	So here's something that you have to figure out. Here's something
that you have to ask yourself. How often do I want my customers to buy from
me again? How often do I want my customers to buy from me again?
So when I was in the menswear business we looked at--we wanted to see a
customer every six months. If I was in auto repair, I'm going to guess once
every three months. If I'm in the restaurant business how often do I want to see
you again? If I don't see somebody within 30 days they are now a lost
customer. Dental practice, what do they tell you? Every six months you've got
to come in for a checkup, right?
So one of the things you've got to think about is when is somebody lost to you?
It's a very important number that you've got to know. Most people don't think
about it enough. As a matter of fact, most people don't think about lost
customers enough, period. And the reason why you really want to think about
them enough is because they are the third easiest customer to get to come
back to your business. Now by the way, when I use the word “customer” it's
interchangeable with “patients” and “clients.” But you want to think about is the
third easiest person to get to come back to your business. Number one is
what? Number one is the what?

-- 6 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 7
Notes
Audience: Current client.
Bill: 	The current customer that you've got. The current customer. What's
number two?
Audience: Referral.
Bill: 	Referral. Somebody who refers to you is the second easiest customer to
get. But the third easiest one to get is the one who was a previous customer
who hasn't come back when they were expected to come back. The sad thing
about this is: where do most people spend most of their time? New customers.
New 	customers 	cost 	at 	least 	five 	times 	more 	to 	attract 	than 	previous
customers. So you've got to figure out, as I said, when a customer is lost
you've got to be thinking that to yourself, when do I stop seeing a student in my
martial arts school? At what point are they now lost?
So what most people do is put their lost customers in their general marketing
campaigns. They just dump them all into their general stuff and they treat them
like they treat active customers, but the better result you'll get is when you let
them actually know that we miss seeing you. And also, the best method to get
customers to respond, number one is determine when they're lost. Number two
is let them know that they are lost. And number three is use something called
“sequential marketing”.
When I first started doing this in my menswear stores, I only used one media
which was what? Direct mail. And then I added voice broadcast. And now (if I
were still in that business) I would probably substitute voice broadcast for email
marketing, but I would use different media because different people respond to
different media, and quite frankly the more different media that you do use,
usually the better the result because different people do respond to different
media.
Just letting them know they're lost is not good enough. You've got to always
use your marketing direct response strategies.

-- 7 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 8
Notes
So you have to have an offer, a deadline, a guarantee, a headline to grab their
attention, testimonials to prove. What are the best testimonials to use in lost
customer 	reactivation 	campaigns? 	Testimonials 	from 	people 	who 	were
previously lost. You show testimonials that, you know, I foolishly stopped
getting my car repaired at ABC Auto Repair and it was the biggest mistake I
ever made. Because I ignored my car, it actually wound up costing me $3,800
in unnecessary repairs that had I taken my car back to them every three
months I could have avoided that. Now that message works in any business at
all. So a testimonial that actually speaks to the fact of the person who was
previously lost and the problems that were created by that. Personalization,
grabbers, premiums, photos, of course the Copy Doodles. So you want to use
direct response techniques in all of this marketing.
So lost customer reactivation examples.

-- 8 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 9
Notes
Omaha Steaks. This was a self-mailer that I got this in the mail and when you
open it up, there was an offer for $20 of a free gift. There was an offer with a
deadline in there, but what's important about this is we want you back and I'll
make it worth your while. What did they identify to me? They identified to me
that I am lost. I am a lost customer. It wasn't the kind of mail that just their
regular customers would get; it's somebody who hasn't bought from them in the
time that they expected me to buy from them again.
Here's another example. Golf magazine reinstatement mailing sent this out.
This is not -- I don't happen to golf, but this is just an example of their thing,
which actually is a reinstatement form to reinstate this person -- thank you --
thank you very much -- reinstate this person Golf subscription. I show you

-- 9 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 10
Notes
these big, big companies for a reason. If they're doing it, it must be worthwhile
to do it and I can assure you it's going to be worth your while to do it as well.
Here's another one. Priceline.com. You get a $50 bonus cash certificate email.
This is an email that goes out to people that have purchased from Priceline in
the past, but when triggered when they haven't had a purchase again they
send this out to them with -- you see there, it says we miss you.

-- 10 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 11
Notes
Here's another one. This is from Free Conference Calling. We want you back.
Do you see a trend to this? They're identifying the fact that the person is
missing. The person gets this, they'll be thinking to themselves, my God,
number one is they care enough about me to come after me and number two is
I should be buying from them again -- because it's not their job to remember to
buy again, it's your job to remind them.
This is like the campaign that was used actually for many, many years,
probably close to six years in my Gage Menswear Store. Now what we would
do is we would mail out to them after somebody hits month number seven. If
they didn't come back by the first six months and it was from the last time they
were there, we would automatically put them into a lost customer reactivation
campaign. 	Consistently 	we 	got 	13% of 	all 	people that 	were 	on 	the 	lost
customer list that month would come back after the campaign. It was a game
changer for us.
The other thing that's important about this is the fact that every month there
was new people that went into this campaign. So if you're looking for a steady
stream of new money, new income coming to your business, it is very smart to
create a lost customer campaign that just keeps rolling month after month after
month.
Now I will tell you, and I'm going to show you the whole campaign, it was a four
step campaign, so don't think it's just we sent out something and all of a
sudden 13% of them came back because you usually have to work harder at
bringing them back with sequential marketing. So a lot of people are going to
be asking themselves what happens to, in this particular case, the 87% that

-- 11 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 12
Notes
didn't come back? What I would do with the 87% that didn't come back in my
menswear stores, which I'd advise all you to do, is wait another period of time,
which I did. Wait another six months, so in month 13 I put them in the same
campaign again and that would bring another 13% of them back to life again.
So sometimes when people don't come back it's just that they're just not ready.
So I would put them in a second campaign. We're going to deal with that
towards the end of this talk to give you really my best advice on how to really
deal with people who just stubbornly will not come back.
But here's the campaign. So the first thing was a six by nine inch postcard that
went out and says, we really do miss you.
We didn't tell them it's been six months just been too long, and we gave them a
free $10 offer just for coming in to the store. That's it. With a deadline. And our
deadline was always two weeks, 14 days was my deadline. We tested a lot of
different deadlines, and this particular campaign, by the way, on this particular
campaign 14 days worked the best and we tested a lot, we tested 10 days, we
tested seven days, we'd actually tested 21 days, 14 days was the best. So
you've got to test different deadlines to see which one works the best. This was
one side of the card; here was the other side of the card.

-- 12 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 13
Notes
By 	the 	way 	you'll 	notice 	that 	it's 	personalized. 	Steve, 	we 	miss 	you.
Personalization will increase the response. If they didn't come back within two
weeks we sent them out a second postcard.

-- 13 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 14
Notes
It's how we sent out the search party. And what's in there is basically the same
as in the first postcard, although we changed the graphics, but there's one key
component part that's in there that you must, must, must do, which is you have
to refer to the fact that approximately two weeks ago we sent you a postcard
with a carrot on it. As you move people down sequential mailing you always
want to refer to the previous mailing that you sent out.
If they didn't respond two weeks later we sent them out the third postcard,
which is the last chance. And in there what we did was previously we sent you
two postcards, one of them with a carrot on it, and the other with a compass on
it and we haven't seen you so we're going to give you an absolute last chance
to come back to us with this special offer. This is one side and here's the other
side. And then we said to them in this postcard if we don't hear from you, you
will never hear from us again.

-- 14 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 15
Notes
Then three days later – we sent them out an email. So back then I used to
send them out a voice broadcast. In this particular case, I would now use an
email. The parts that are highlighted are the parts that just change, but the
email is a basic email that went out to them. And this is the type of campaign,
although back then I was using voice broadcast, would get me 13% response
on a regular basis.

-- 15 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 16
Notes
[Here are examples from another sequential campaign:]

-- 16 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 17
Notes

-- 17 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 18

-- 18 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 19
Notes
Moving from direct mail to the phone. Phone call from the store manager, offer
them a free week of unlimited tanning, they stop in within the next four days.
The phone calls are very well received and the clients greatly appreciate it. If
we get an answering machine we leave a message saying that we have a free
gift for them if they call us back. This is a new step we have just added and it
has been working well. Cost is zero since they're using their existing staff to
make the calls and this stuff is bringing in an additional four to five percent of
the list by adding a different kind of media, which in this particular case is
outbound phone call. And many of you have staff that could actually be doing
these things adding phone. Or use email.
This is from Platinum S my member, Sandy Krakowski. So this is for her
coaching program to reactivate people, and I'll read you a little bit of it here. It
says, "Hey, first name, how's it going? You know life happens doesn't it?

-- 19 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 20
Notes
Things power up and before we know it there's not a few things on the to-do list
that aren't getting done. There's a month worth of projects that we've gotten off
track with. I totally know what this is like and why I spend regular time with my
coaches making sure derailing doesn't happen. The fact of the matter is the
only difference between a millionaire and a want-a-be business owner is they
know how to prevent derailing and getting off track. We're here to support you
and believe in your dreams. Here's a copy of last night's coaching group called
No Charge, my gift to you, Link to the Call. Your dreams are important to me.
Let's get back on track. Link to reactivate coaching. With love, Sandy..."
You guys think that's a good email to go out? That's a spectacular email to go
out. And it's very, very conversational. Very, very conversational and you see
that all the blame was taken off of the fact that they quit and put on other
factors. So again, it's very similar to as I told you the outbound phone call that
we made in my menswear store saying that, hey, listen, I know you didn't make
your time that we had set aside today. I just assume something came up. It's
happened to me also, so let's just get back together again. It takes the blame
off them because most people are embarrassed when they stop doing the
things that you want them to do. So it's a very, very smart strategy to send out
to them. Plus the fact she gave them a free call, which is also a reciprocity
piece.
[Here’s another approach.] Step No. 1 is the brown bag that goes out. I told

-- 20 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 21
Notes
you I put it in the Hall of Fame in the Outrageous talk. A brown bag that goes
out. Inside there's a letter that goes in the brown bag. Step No. 2 is the
postcard, we've been searching for you. And Step No. 3, I actually took the
postcard and created it into a Step No. 3 email.
Helpful hints
Number one. Ask the lost customer what we can do to win you back. Maybe
they didn't come back because they had a bad experience with you. Maybe
they didn't come back because they moved away. Maybe they didn't come
back because your prices were too high. but none of those reasons [matter]
unless you know what the objection is. So if they don't come back for the
regular lost campaign, ask them why they didn't come back. What can we do to
win you back? Then you've got to listen and be open to what lost customers tell
you.
Now you've got to listen to that and you've got to be open to what they tell you
because sometimes you've got to go fix the other problem that's in your
business.
So if now you can meet the requirement or you can overcome their objection,
then ask them to come back again and then at that point you probably need to
do some kind of a special offer to them to bring them back, but they will really,

-- 21 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 22
Notes
really, really thank you for doing that for them and if you win those people back
it's going to be really hard for you to lose those people because you really went
the extra mile, which nobody else does.
Continue to keep in touch with lost customers. What is the best media to keep
in touch with people on a monthly basis? Hint. Newsletters. Everybody should
be in a newsletter with you to keep in touch with them.
Next is have multiple front-end offers for lost customers to return to you.
The most important point about reviving lost customers. What is the overall
most important point about reviving lost customers? It's this. Have a strategic
lost customer reactivation plan in place. So if there's else that I can impress
upon you while you are here is if you want to get back the third easiest
customers to get to your business, what you want to do is you want to as you're
sitting here and seeing these examples that I show you, you want to go through
the process.
Summary
Process number 1 is identifying in your business when are your customers lost.
Number 2 is you've got to let the customers know that they are lost. And
number 3 is you want to put them into a lost customer reactivation campaign
based on when they are lost and you do it multi-step sequentially and you want

-- 22 of 23 --

© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle LLC 	Page 23
Notes
to systemize it. You want to make a system so like I said to you in my
menswear stores, every month whoever hit that number, month number seven,
they got into that campaign.
So that is that story on the lost customer reactivation campaign.
End of presentation.

-- 23 of 23 --
